label: no
cot_prefix: Okay, so there's this problem about a teacher and four students. The teacher picks four consecutive two-digit numbers, gives each student a different number from that set. The teacher tells them that there's a number divisible by 6 and another divisible by 7 in the set. Then she asks if any of them can figure out what the set S is. All four students say no. But after hearing that all four said no, they can figure it out. The question is asking for the sum of all possible values of the greatest element of S. First, let me parse the problem again. The set S has four consecutive two-digit numbers. So numbers like 10-13, 11-14, ..., up to maybe 97-100? But since they are two-digit numbers, the maximum would be 97, 98, 99, 100? Wait, but 100 is a three-digit number. So the four consecutive numbers must be from 10 up to such that the fourth number is still two-digit. So the highest set is 97, 98, 99, 100? But 100 is three-digit. Therefore, actually the four consecutive numbers must be up to 96-99? Because 96, 97, 98, 99. Then the next one would be 97, 98, 99, 100, but 100 is three-digit. Therefore, the maximum possible set is 96-99. So the greatest element of S can be up to 99. So S is a set of four consecutive two-digit numbers. Each student gets one number. The teacher told them that in S, there is some number divisible by 6 and a different number divisible by 7. So there are two different numbers in S: one divisible by 6, another divisible by 7. Then the teacher asks if any of the students can figure out S. All four students say no. Then, upon hearing that all four said no, they can figure out S. We need to find all such possible sets S, take their maximum elements, and sum them. So first, I need to figure out all possible sets of four consecutive two-digit numbers that contain a multiple of 6 and a multiple of 7. Then, among those, figure out which ones have the property that when each student knows their own number and the above information, they all say "no" initially, but then can deduce S after hearing that everyone said no. This seems like a logic puzzle where we need to use the concept of common knowledge and iterated elimination. Let me recall similar problems. For example, the blue-eyed islanders problem, or the "sum and product" problem. In those, the key is that each person's knowledge and their knowledge about others' knowledge leads to elimination of possibilities. In this case, each student knows their own number and knows that the set is four consecutive numbers with a multiple of 6 and a multiple of 7. Since they are logical, they can try to figure out what the set is based on their number and the given conditions.
